gapped_text:
What particularly impresses Judith about many forms of _______ communication is the degree of ingenuity shown by the people who devised them. Ancient civilisations were not ignorant of the fact that an effective communication network was a fundamental part of _______ a vast empire.
The Roman _______ _______ was the most advanced of its time.
The Romans relied upon a _______ of messengers for speed of delivery.
A boom in international trade in the Middle Ages brought about an increase in _______ _______ leading to a reformation of existing postal services.
These reforms were later improved upon as a result of the invention of the _______ _______ .
The failure of the Pony Express was attributed to the invention of the _______ .
The fact that pigeons are well-suited for _______ was well-known by the ancient Egyptians.
More recently, carrier pigeons proved themselves to be invaluable as _______ _______ .

Antibiotics are ineffective against _______ infections.
Infections are triggered by two kinds of _______ .
Antibiotics are only useful when it comes to fighting _______ bacteria.
When bacteria _______ _______ to antibiotics they become resistant.
Unnecessary use of antibiotics renders one vulnerable to _______ _______ .
Infections caused by bacteria are extremely _______ .
According to the World Health Organisation, proper use of antibiotics results in the maximum clinical _______ _______ .
For doctors, getting the dosage and duration of an antibiotic prescription right is quite a _______ _______ .
Some foods interfere with the _______ of antibiotics in the stomach.

Robert Lee runs an organisation that specialises in the _______ of professionals.
Robert is certain that it will not be possible to succeed in the e-economy without possessing _______ _______ .
According to Robert, more people will use the Internet to work _______ as companies grow smaller.
Employers of e-lancers benefit from the talent auction because it gives them a(n) _______ _______ of candidates.
Robert says that those people who want to and are able to _______ will be most in demand in the future.
Research has shown that increased _______ can ensure better learning choices.
According to Robert, it is not the subject, but the actual _______ of learning that is important.
As a result of changes in the working environment, managers will need to _______ .
Nowadays, the amount of time employees spend on their work is less important than their _______ .

Impression management is the science of _______ appropriately to another person’s body language.
An open posture involves a speaker standing _______ his audience.
An open posture projects _______ _______ to listeners.
Crossed legs and _______ arms are characteristics of a closed posture.
A closed posture suggests that the person is afraid of _______ .
_______ _______ when listening indicates that the person is accepting what you are saying, and that he is ready to _______ affirmatively .
When someone stares at the ceiling, it may be a good idea to _______ _______ for a little or end the discussion.
Observations of people under _______ _______ have shown that most of us do not understand the basics of impression management.

Mercury Theatre's _______ was a programme entitled 'Chase and Sanborn Hour'.
Welles' adaptation of War of the Worlds involved shortening the story and _______ the setting.
Welles came on air and those who were tuned in heard him setting _______ _______ .
A(n) _______ _______ interrupted the music to announce the sightings of explosions on Mars.
The script was such that the interview with the astronomer sounded particularly _______ .
Many listeners missed the announcements that the programme was only a(n) _______ story.
The commentary and interviews sounded so real and _______ that people who had missed the announcements had no idea they were just a story.
Some people improvised gas masks; there were even reports of some early births and _______ .
This incident shows how _______ _______ _______ _______ can mislead audiences.

Always _______ on the right. Move into the outside lane.
If you need to stop, let the other drivers know by putting your _______ lights on.
Carefully move onto the hard shoulder or the lay-by.
To leave a motorway, take the slip road. Once on the slip road, _______ speed immediately.
When executing these _______ , never forget to _______ .
Respect the 70 mph speed _______ .
Motorists caught _______ have to pay heavy _______ .
Be considerate and polite; give _______ as often as you can. Drive _______ , and never assume other drivers are as careful as you.

Mir's mission was to provide Russian cosmonauts with a _______ .
Mir remained _______ _______ for nine years longer than had been originally planned.
Only when oxygen started leaking into space was Mir finally _______ _______ .
_______ to the suggestion that Mir be scrapped was significant.
A Dutch company envisaged a future for Mir as a _______ _______ .
One person invested $1 million in preparation for the _______ which would make him the first space tourist ever.
Few people can endure being _______ _______ for prolonged period of times.
Returning to Earth, some cosmonauts are so weak that they can't even come out of their _______ .
_______ _______ is described as by far the worst kind of travel sickness.

In order to shorten the walk, the speaker crossed some agricultural parts of the country by car, as they were _______ _______ .
Whichever part of a map of England you look at, there will probably be a(n) _______ _______ very nearby.
Wainwright felt that people enjoyed long-distance walks because of the _______ they offered.
He thought the Coast-to-Coast Walk was better than the Pennine Way because of its _______ _______ .
In the mountains, visibility is sometimes excellent, but the scenery can very quickly become _______ _______ _______ .
Overnight accommodation is available at _______ _______ .
Wainwright’s guide to the walk includes _______ _______ and relevant parts of maps.
When planning a route on this walk, detailed Ordnance Survey Maps are _______ .
The majority of mountain ascent paths on the walk begin _______ _______ _______ .

Sean Flint reports that the tradition of psychic healing dates back to _______ _______ _______ _______ _______ .
The miraculous cures wrought by psychic surgeons brought _______ _______ _______ to sufferers and their families.
There is a general misconception that faith healers _______ _______ for their work.
A man in California was charged with posing as a doctor and the illegal _______ _______ _______ .
Cotton balls are used to generate the illusion of _______ when fake operations are being performed.
The rumours surrounding the efficacy of psychic surgeons are spread by disreputable _______ and word of mouth.
_______ _______ are brought on by extended periods of stress.
Often _______ _______ _______ is ever discovered for persistent pain in people suffering from psychosomatic illnesses.
The fallacy concerning miracle cures is also _______ by the people who attribute their return to health to psychic surgeons.

Jonathan states that "A Love Supreme" and some other albums he listened to in his childhood were to him the _______ _______ _______ _______ .
In his music, John Coltrane works with some very complicated _______ _______ , but one doesn't have to understand those in order to appreciate it.
According to Jonathan, Coltrane did not care about _______ _______ when he played music.
Jonathan says that some people who do not normally listen to jazz, will be able to _______ to it if they are not made aware that it is actually free jazz.
The music Jonathan wrote for the theatrical play aims to place the listener in a _______ _______ _______ .
Jonathan went to the play's rehearsals and even watched videos of the rehearsals to ensure that there was _______ between his composition and the play.
The reason Jonathan gives for never being able to shake off Coltrane's influence is because music is part of _______ _______ .
Four out of the twelve pieces on Jonathan's new album are _______ _______ .
Jonathan has not changed his style much and is still _______ _______ playing easy-going jazz.

Visitors to the New York Marathon website are expected to reach a(n) _______ number this year.
The recent Olympics showed that having a website running alongside TV coverage is a very important _______ _______ _______ .
_______ _______ _______ will allow the website's visitors to follow their favourite athlete through the marathon.
Once the web site knows the name of a visitor's favourite athlete, _______ _______ about his or her performance become available.
At various points in the race, a(n) _______ _______ developed in the Netherlands will record runners' times.
The content appearing on the viewer's computer will be automatically _______ .
On the day of the marathon, the organisers are expecting a tremendous _______ of Web traffic.
Especially as runners approach the finish line, the website will get _______ .
With new advances in Internet technology, such websites will be able to send out much more than _______ _______ .

World War I, which began in 1914 and ended in 1918, is sometimes called _______ _______ _______.
In 1928, British women obtained the _______ after a long battle.
The _______ _______ _______ in America in 1929 had far-reaching social consequences in the US and abroad.
On August 6th 1945 the _______ _______ almost totally destroyed Hiroshima.
On April 12th 1961 Yuri Gagarin _______ _______ in a rocketship, marking the beginning of the space age.
In 1964 Martin Luther King won the _______ _______ _______ for his role in the civil rights movement in the USA.

The interviewer comments that prejudice is usually _______ _______ unless it stems from some sort of shocking encounter.
When people feel that only their way is acceptable, they _______ _______ any other way of thinking or acting.
Solidarity can be beneficial, but at times it can result in people creating _______ _______ about others.
The prejudice reduction workshops advocate the theory that discrimination requires both a purpose and a(n) _______.
Prejudice arises when a group feels _______ _______ _______ in relation to others.
In the initial stage of the workshops, a request is made for participants _______ _______ on their inner emotions and their feelings regarding others.
One member of the pair tries to evoke a spontaneous _______ _______ from his partner through the repetition of a particular word.
Putting people into large groups gives them the chance to talk about their reactions and any _______ _______ they might possess.
On completion of the workshop, an objective individual should be able to _______ _______ and come to terms with their hostility.

Dr Novak describes space junk as being a result of our own _______ negligence.
Government agencies are now joined by _______ enterprises in space exploration.
Discarded spacecraft and equipment constitute a kind of _______ time capsule.
In the 1960s, one astronaut _______ _______ _______ on his camera in orbit.
In space, the paint on spacecraft has a _______ _______ _______.
Given time, _______ _______ _______ or are hit and penetrated by debris.
When explosions in space become less frequent, spacecraft will start being destroyed _______ _______.
The number of pieces of debris will _______ _______ once cascading begins.
Sustainable development must not affect the ability of our descendants to _______ _______ _______.
